coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots based single-luminophor generated dual-potential electrochemiluminescence system for ratiometric sensing of Co2+ ion
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hongjun
Li, Wang
Wang, Qin
Jin, Xin
Nie, Zhou
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, a single luminophor dual-potential chemiluminescence (ECL) system based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) was developed for ratiometric detection of Co2+ ion. Dissolved oxygen is changed to O2
− and HO2 − which participate in the anodic and cathodic ECL generating processes of NGQDs. Co2+ ion was demonstrated able to evidently amplify the anodic intensity by displaying catalytic action of the intermediate steps of anodic ECL reactions, and quench the cathodic ECL intensity because of the effective elimination on excited states (NGQDs*) generating. Hence, the target Co2+ ion could be quantified by the ratio of ECL intensities at two excitation potentials. On this basis, without any labeling and additional coreactants, an accurate and reliable sensing system based on the dual-potential ECL of NGQDs toward Co2+ ion was fabricated with excellent sensitivity and selectivity against other interferential metal ions. The developed ratiometric sensor exhibited a linear response ranging from 1.0μM to 70μM with a limit of detection of 0.2μM (S/N
=
3). The proposed dual-potential ratiometric NGQDs based ECL sensor was also used to detect Co2+ ion in real water samples. As single luminophor, NGQDs possessing multiple-ECL signals is simple without labeling, providing more reliable signals in sensing than other essential state dependent techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
single-luminophor
dual-potential electrochemiluminescence
ratiometric
Co2+ ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigations on the influence of pH
Cobalt ion modulated dual-potential ECL properties of NGQDs
ECL response of the dual-signal ratiometric sensor to Co2+ ion concentration
Detection of Co2+ in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575038 , 21235002 , and 21305037 ), the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of NSFC (Grant 21521063 ), Young Top-notch Talent for Ten Thousand Talent Program , the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2015JJ1005 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)Typical TEM image and (B) high-resolutionTEM image of the prepared NGQDs, and (C) fast Fourier transform image of the selected NGQDs as shown in part B. (D) UV–vis spectra of the GO and NGQDs (The inset shows the photograph of the NGQDs under excitation of 365nm.) (E) Wide scan XPS spectrum of N-GQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of the N1s (F) and C1s (G) in NGQDs, respectively, and their related curve-fitted components.

(A) ECL-potential curve of NGQDs (inset: CV response without NGQDs (black line) in 0.2M air-saturated PBS; and with NGQDs before (red line) and after (blue line) in PBS bubbled with N2). (B) ECL-time curve of the NGQDs under continuous scanning for 9 cycles in 0.2M air saturated PBS solution (pH=8.0). (C) ECL peak spectra of anodic responses (blue line) and cathodic responses (red line) respectively, obtained by the optical filter (inset shows the normalized photoluminescence spectrum of NGQDs).

(A) ECL responses of NGQDs before (black line) and after (red line) being bubbled with nitrogen and exposed to air (blue line). (B) ECL responses of NGQDs in the presence (black line) and absence (red line) of 20μgmL−1 SOD. (C) ECL responses of NGQDs before (black line) and after (red line) the addition of 50μgmL−1 catalase. (D) ECL-potential curve of NGQDs in 0.2M air saturated PBS solution with different pH values.

Synchronously ECL-potential (A) and CV curves (B) of NGQDs (black line), under the conditions that mixed with 100μM cobalt ions (red line), and 100μM EDTA with the coexistence of equal cobalt ions (blue line). (C) Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of NGQDs before (black line) and after being added with 100μM (red line) and 1mM (blue line) Co2+ ion. The excitation wavelength of PL spectrum was 365nm.

Cathodic (A) and anodic (B) ECL intensities of NGQDs obtained with the presence of various metal ions (100μM) respectively (note: the Ic 0 , Ia 0 is the initial cathodic and anodic ECL intensity of NGQDs). (C) Ratiometric result derived from the changes between anodic (B) and cathodic (A) potential.

(A) ECL-potential responses of NGQDs toward different concentrations of Co2+ ion in 0.2M PBS solution (pH=8.0) and (B) the ratiometric linear fitting relationship between Ia/Ic and the square concentration of Co2+ ion.
A comparison of the performance of various analytical methods for detection of Co2+.

row.entry: combination with double cloud point extraction
combination with online cloud point extraction
cloud point extraction using methyl-2-pyridylketone oxime

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Co2+ ion in real water samples using the NGQDs based ECL system.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots based single-luminophor generated dual-potential electrochemiluminescence system for ratiometric sensing of Co2+ ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, a single luminophor dual-potential chemiluminescence (ECL) system based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) was developed for ratiometric detection of Co2+ ion. Dissolved oxygen is changed to O2
− and HO2 − which participate in the anodic and cathodic ECL generating processes of NGQDs. Co2+ ion was demonstrated able to evidently amplify the anodic intensity by displaying catalytic action of the intermediate steps of anodic ECL reactions, and quench the cathodic ECL intensity because of the effective elimination on excited states (NGQDs*) generating. Hence, the target Co2+ ion could be quantified by the ratio of ECL intensities at two excitation potentials. On this basis, without any labeling and additional coreactants, an accurate and reliable sensing system based on the dual-potential ECL of NGQDs toward Co2+ ion was fabricated with excellent sensitivity and selectivity against other interferential metal ions. The developed ratiometric sensor exhibited a linear response ranging from 1.0μM to 70μM with a limit of detection of 0.2μM (S/N
=
3). The proposed dual-potential ratiometric NGQDs based ECL sensor was also used to detect Co2+ ion in real water samples. As single luminophor, NGQDs possessing multiple-ECL signals is simple without labeling, providing more reliable signals in sensing than other essential state dependent techniques.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) generated by an oxidation or reduction reaction at electrode surface has attracted considerable attention due to its inherent advantages such as high sensitivity and low background signals [1–3]. However, traditional ECL methods were fabricated based on single emission intensity changes, which usually confronted with some inevitable challenges of intensity fluctuations resulted from instrumental or environmental factors, especially with low concentrations of target or in complex biological systems. Resorting on ratiometric methods, these problems could be overcome by virtue of dual-response ECL detection. Hence, developing dual-signal ECL through potential scanning is feasible because of the controllability of emission from ECL emitter in the given potential range. In recent studies, ECL ratiometric sensing approaches are typically designed based on the following two principles: (1) double luminophors including CdS quantum dots, Ru(bpy)3 2+, luminol or graphene quantum dots with the aid of coreactant such as tripropylamine (TPA), H2O2 or persulfate [4–9]; (2) one luminophor with two different kinds of coreactants [10]. Elaborate packages on the two kinds of luminophors and requirement for large amount of coreactant are practically necessary to these proposed dual-signal ECL system, which would limit its practical applications. Consequently, it is of great significance to discover single luminophor material that can produce dual-signal ECL intensities without adding extra coreatants.

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) possess marvelous properties such as photoluminescence, high electrocatalytic activity and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) associated with their quantum confinement and edge effects [11–13]. Tremendous attentions have been paid for the applications of their photoluminescence and electrocatalytic activity [13–15]. Nowadays, limited studies are implemented in terms of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a cathodic or anodic ECL reagent for sensing construction [16,17]. However, investigations of intrinsic dual-potential properties of NGQDs are rare. Herein, NGQDs emitter possessing simultaneous cathodic and anodic ECL properties in one ECL system is developed. More surprising, the dual-potential ECL response is generated only with help of the dissolved oxygen rather than other extra coreactant.

As a key component of vitamin B12, cobalt plays an important role in humans and biological systems [18]. Notably, cobalt is also acutely toxic in large doses and even long-term exposure to trace levels. In daily life, humans may be exposed to cobalt compounds from diet and occupational exposure such as porcelain, the hard metal and chemical [19]. Shedding light on these aspects, the development of highly sensitive and selective analytical methodology of Co2+ ion is conducive to avoid its toxic effects. So far, numerous typical techniques such as colorimetric [20], chemiluminescent [21], plasma optical emission spectrometric [22] and fluorescent methods [23,24] are applied for cobalt sensing. However, these methods have their own limitations such as the requirement of expensive instrument, tedious sample pretreatment, and being time-consuming.

In this work, a dual-potential ratiometric responsive ECL sensor was fabricated for Co2+ ion detection. Herein, NGQDs as single luminophor employing simultaneous dual-potential ECL response is synthesized by cutting nitrogen doped graphene oxide (NGO) to small size and then reduced to form NGQDs. NGQDs emit two ECL signals at both positive and negative potentials in the participation of dissolved oxygen. The mechanisms of the dual-potential response are discussed in details as well. In the presence of Co2+ ion, ECL intensity of NGQDs increased dramatically (amplified about 15 times) in the anodic potential range and decreased evidently in the cathodic potential range. On the basis of the ratio of two ECL intensities, a ratiometric sensor for Co2+ ion is developed and has been applied to detection of Co2+ in real water. As a single luminophor, NGQDs possessing multiple-ECL signals is convenient without labeling, providing more reliable signals in sensing compared to traditional single ECL signal dependent strategies, which would expand the single emitter induced dual-potential ECL application.

Graphite powder (99.9%, 325 meshes) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase were gained from Sigma-Aldrich. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS, pH 8.0) were prepared using 0.2M KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Hg2+ ion is derived from Hg(NO3)2, while the other metal ions are used with their chlorides. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q system (18.2MΩ·cm) was used in all runs.

The electrochemical and ECL measurements were carried out on an MPI-A multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system (Remex, Xi’an, China) at room temperature with a configuration consisting of an indium/tin oxide (ITO) working electrode, a platinum counter electrode, and a Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl solution) reference electrode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al Kα (1486.6eV) as the X-ray source. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F30 ST instruments (FEI Co., U.S.A.) operating at 300kV. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a 1cm path length quartz cuvette on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on Beckman Coulter DU-800.

NGQDs were achieved by reduction of NGO. Briefly, the nanometer-sized NGO (1mgmL−1) was initially fabricated with ammonium hydroxide and H2O2 according to the reported method [14]. Then, 10mL of the as prepared homogeneous brown dispersion substance was reduced using hydrazine by a previously reported hydrothermal method [13,15]. After cooling to room temperature, the large sheets were filtered out with 0.22μm millipore membrane and the desired claybank supernatant NGQDs were collected. The supernatant was dispersed in ethanol and rotary evaporation was used to vaporise excessive hydrazine and then freeze-dried. Finally, the frozen substance was configured to the solution with water and stored at 4°C for an imperative aging process before use.

MPI-A multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system was used to detect the ECL emissions throughout the experiments and the emission window was set at 750V. ECL emission of NGQDs was investigated by scanning the potential initially from 0V to 1.25V, then reversed to −0.75V, finally return to 0V using ITO as working electrode. The detection solution was fixed to 400μL with PBS containing 2μgmL−1 NGQDs solutions. The ECL spectrum was recorded by collecting the ECL data at +1.24V and −0.75V respectively during cyclic dual-potential sweep with nine pieces of long-pass filters at cutoff wavelengths of 360, 380, 400, 420, 450, 470, 490, 510 and 550nm, and the data were fitted in accordance with reported literature [25].

The as-prepared NGQDs can disperse in water easily and exhibit a long-term homogeneous phase without any evident precipitation at room temperature. TEM image (Fig. 1 A) shows a fairly uniform morphology with diameters ranging from 4 to 6nm, providing the first confirmation of formed nanosize pieces during the synthesis of NGQDs. Moreover, high-resolution TEM image reveals excellent crystallinity with a lattice parameter of 0.21nm (Fig. 1B) of NGQDs, which agrees with the in-plane lattice fringes (100) of graphene [13]. The fast Fourier transform image of the lattice fringes in Fig. 1C further reveals the crystalline hexagonal structures of NGQDs [26].

Once the synthesis of the nanosize NGQDs sheets was confirmed, the spectroscopic and optical characteristics of the NGQDs were studied. Under irradiation by a 365nm lamp (16W), the NGQDs emit intense blue luminescence (inset of Fig. 1D). In addition, NGQDs exhibit a broad UV–vis absorption below 500nm with a shoulder at 374nm and a peak at 224nm (Fig. 1D), similar to other nitrogen doped graphene material [14]. The strong adsorption peak at 224nm is assigned to π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, and is blue-shifted by ca. 10nm compared with that of graphene oxide (GO) which is arising from withdrawing electronic effect of the nitrogen atoms incorporated in the carbon framework of graphene [13]. The broad adsorption peak centered at 374nm displays a characteristic absorption band of C-N and is derived from the n–π* transition of C−N, further indicating the doping of N atoms [13,26].

XPS is a proficient and frequently used tool to investigate the features of elementary species in carbon materials [27], and is performed to determine the composition of the as-produced NGQDs. Fig. 1E depicts C1s peak at ∼284.8eV and O1s peak at ∼531.8eV for NGQDs. And the objective N1s peak (at ∼400.9eV) confirms the successful incorporation of N atoms into graphene [13,26,28]. The deconvolution of the N1s peak (Fig. 1F) gives more elaborate information about nitrogen functional groups upon NGQDs. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of NGQDs indicates the presence of∼54% pyridinic N (∼399.9eV) and 46% graphitic N (∼401.5eV) atoms [26,27,29]. Fig. 1G is the deconvolution of C 1s peak which exhibits a dominant C–C bond (284.6eV) and some O-rich groups, such as C−O (285.8eV), CO (288.2eV) bonds [30,31].

ECL emission of NGQDs was investigated in air saturated PBS using ITO as working electrode. In this system, two ECL emission signals are observed at both anode and cathode. For the positive potential scanning, NGQDs exhibit an intense ECL emission at 1.24V with an onset potential at 0.8V (Fig. 2 A), and generate an irreversible redox peak (red line in the inset of Fig. 2A) ascribing to electrochemical oxidation of NGQDs. For the negative potential scanning, once the potential scans over −0.35V, the NGQDs exhibit a quick current increment (red line in the inset of Fig. 2A) and initially emit light with a strongest cathodic ECL emission at −0.75V (Fig. 2A). There is no CV peak that obtained on the condition of without NGQDs (black line in the inset of Fig. 2A), and the current is remarkably lower than the obtained current values in the presence of NGQDs (red line in the inset of Fig. 2A). In addition, when the oxygen was expelled from the detection solution bubbling with nitrogen, the CV currents decreased (blue line in the inset of Fig. 2A). Although other carbon materials were ECL-active at either cathodic or anodic potential ranges with the aid of coreactant such as H2O2, S2O8 2− and TPA [10,16,17], this kind of NGQDs emitting ECL at both of cathodic and anodic potential simultaneously without the requirement of any additional coreactants has never been reported so far. It is worth drawing efforts to uncover its unique ECL mechanism and potential applications in field of sensing fabrication. In addition, NGQDs preserve stable ECL intensity (Fig. 2B) with a relative standard deviation of 3.6% and 1.3% at the anodic and cathodic potential ranges respectively even after 9 cycles of sequential voltage scans, demonstrating good stability and reliability for ECL reactions and promising applications for sensor construction. Especially, the cathodic emission of NGQDs changes in the same way as the anodic emission (Fig. 2C), and both of two potential ECL peaks are similarly to the PL emitting of NGQDs (inset of Fig. 2C) indicating that the emitter of the ECL emission is the excited state of NGQDs (NGQDs*), which is the same as the PL procedure [32,33].

It is significant to disclose reasonable mechanism toward the dual-potential responsive system. Considering the dual-potential ECL phenomena of NGQDs are generated in air-saturated solution, especially without supernumerary coreactants, the effects of dissolved oxygen on the dual-potential emission intensities were initially investigated. Once the detection solution was bubbled with N2, both anodic and cathodic ECL emission intensities decreased dramatically (Fig. 3 A). Besides, upon oxygen free solution being exposed to air again, the ECL intensities recover in two potentials (the blue line in Fig. 3A). Therefore, the dissolved oxygen actually played an imperative role in the ECL emitting procedures. Most notably, tiny enhancing action is observed even though in pure oxygen saturated buffer, indicating that the dissolved oxygen is sufficient for producing stable ECL in conventional air-saturated solution. Superoxide radical (including O2
− and HO2 ) is commonly generated from oxygen in an electrochemical process and able to participate in ECL emission due to its strong electron or hole injection properties. Hence, the removal of superoxide radical during the dual-potential scanning was explored by the addition of excess superoxide dismutase (SOD) owing to its elimination capability against superoxide radical. As shown in Fig. 3B, ECL intensity synchronously decreases a large extent between two potential ECL processes, proving superoxide radicals are actually rising up during the processes. O2
− is typically reported being generated easily from dissolved oxygen by trapping an electron on the ITO surface (Eq. (2)) and able to participate in quantum dots based anodic ECL procedure such as CdTe and graphene oxide quantum dots [34–36]. Alternatively, it is reasonable to consider that O2
− is most possibly generated during positive scanning and involved in anodic ECL emissive procedure [25,34–36]. With regard to anodic ECL procedure, thus, the mechanism can be speculated that NGQDs are electrochemically oxidized to form cationic radical species (NGQDs +, Eq. (1)), the electrochemically generated O2
− concomitants inject an electron to the conduction band of NGQDs + producing light-emitting excitation states of NGQDs (NGQDs*, Eq. (3)), the NGQDs* finally get right back to ground state and emit light (Eq. (4)). As to cathodic potential, under alkali conditions, HO2 − can be generated resorting two electron transfer mechanism during oxygen reducing procedure (Eq. (6)) [37,38]. Consequently, an increased current occurs when the potential scans negative over −0.35V as shown in Fig. 2A, suggesting the formation of HO2 − [39,40]. Serving as a strong oxidizing reagent, HO2 − possesses the capability to inject holes into anionic radical species quantum dots during cathodic ECL processes [41,42]. Furthermore, catalase shows negligible effect (Fig. 3C) upon the cathodic ECL intensities of NGQDs, which is ruled out from the effect of hydrogen peroxide generated during electrochemical reduction reaction of oxygen as reported in other papers [43,44]. In view of cathodic potential, the mechanism can be presumed that NGQDs are directly electrochemically reduced through formation of anionic radical species (NGQDs −, Eq. (5)), then the concomitant strong oxidant HO2 − injects holes to valance band of NGQDs − producing NGQDs* (Eq. (7)), thus finally emits light (Eq. (8)). In general, the ECL reactions are correlated with the electrochemical potentials imposed on the electrode. Consequently, we believe that the dual-potential ECL mechanism of NGQDs involves the dissolved oxygen and generates ECL emission process as the proposed pathway. The entire dual-potential ECL mechanism of NGQDs with respect to both anodic and cathodic processes is summarized as the following equations (1) ∼ (8).

Anodic Process
(1)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigations on the influence of pH
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, as the operational stability is crucial for the dual-potential ECL system in terms of practical application, the effects of electrolyte pH value on the anodic and cathodic ECL intensity of NGQDs were studied (Fig. 3D). None of anodic and cathodic ECL emission is observed at pH 6.0. Notably, both of the two potential ECL intensities grow with the increasing pH from 6.0 to 9.0. It is significant to pay sufficient attention to the reason for such an interesting phenomenon. According to the ECL mechanism of NGQDs, O2
− and HO2 − species are generated at the anodic and cathodic potential emissions respectively, while basic ambient condition is conducive to their formation and stability, therefore the more caustic the solution, the more favorable for the ECL process that leads to a stronger ECL intensity [34]. Considering suitable ECL intensities and moderate pH is beneficial to sensor fabrication, pH 8.0 was selected as the implementing optimum condition in following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cobalt ion modulated dual-potential ECL properties of NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt is a vital trace element which plays an important role in humans and biological systems. Toxicological effects would be activated when humans and animals expose to cobalt [18]. Thus, it is crucial to detect trace amounts of cobalt in environmental samples. To our surprising, upon addition of cobalt ion to the electrolyte, ECL intensity increased about 15 times in the anodic potential range and decreased obviously in the cathodic potential range (Fig. 4 A). The decrement in NGQDs* derived from suppressive formation of HO2 − by Co2+could be the main reason of the decrease of cathodic current. The cathodic ECL decrease could also be ascribed to the quenching effects on the excited NGQDs* by the pathway where the excited electrons of NGQDs were accepted by Co2+ ion instead of transferring back to the ground state of NGQDs. The quenching effect for excited state can also be verified by the fact that the fluorescence of NGQDs decreases with the coexistence of Co2+ ion (Fig. 4C). More inspiring, ECL intensity of NGQDs in the presence of Co2+ ion was improved evidently in anodic intensity instead of the conventional quenching. It was also noted that the electrochemical current increases dramatically with the presence of Co2+ ion in the anodic potential range (Fig. 4 B the red line), suggesting a faster electron transfer during the anodic ECL reactions [37,45]. The specific ECL amplification would derive from the catalytic action of Co2+ ion which accelerates the anodic ECL reactions (see Eqs. (1) ∼ (4)), as reported in other papers [21,45]. The reaction of Eq. (4) occurs spontaneously because the excitation states of NGQDs are at higher energy beneficial to output photon quickly [10]. In this regard, enhancement of the ECL intensity of NGQDs after the addition of Co2+ ion was presumably due to the catalytic action of reaction Eqs. (1) ∼ (3). Furthermore, once the Co2+ ion is chelated with equimolar EDTA (Fig. 4B the blue curve), the anodic current decreased clearly and the ECL decreased being comparable to that of without Co2+ ion, verified Co2+ ion would act as catalytic interactions of the intermediate species in ECL reactions. To the best of our knowledge, such unique ECL enhancement and quenching on Co2+ ion at different potentials for NGQDs have never been reported so far, although the individual cathodic [17] and anodic [16] ECL reactions of graphene material were studied previously.

To evaluate the selectivity of the dual-potential system, the effects of common metal cations on the ECL intensity were investigated (Fig. 5 ). It was found that the ECL intensity decreased to different extents for most metal ion in the cathodic potential range (Fig. 5A). It could be ascribed to the quenching effect of metal ions, especially those usually photo/electrochemical luminescent quenching ions such as Fe2+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ ions [24,46]. For the anodic potential range, the ECL intensity of NGQDs in the presence of Co2+ improves evidently while other metal ions exhibit small amplifications effect or remain the same intensity (Fig. 5B), suggesting the special selectivity in anodic ECL process. As shown in Fig. 5A, the ECL intensity was quenched in the presence of Co2+, Ni2+ or Cu2+ ion in the cathodic ECL signal, it is hard to distinguish Co2+ from Ni2+ or Cu2+ ion and other metal ions coexisted in the sample if only based on the single cathodic ECL signal. As for anodic ECL signal, the signal ratio of target-to-interference (SRoTI) is about 7 for Ni2+ ion and 15 for Cu2+ ion. In light of this dual-signal ECL emission, the interference from other metal ion to the target ion could be largely avoided. In principle, compared to a single-ECL signal, the SRoTI can reach to 12 and 19 times for Ni2+ and Cu2+ ion respectively, and exhibiting 75-120 times for other ions (Fig. 5C). Therefore, a more sensitive and convinced ratiometric result with high discrimination is stimulated, endowing considerable capability of resisting disturbance against other metal ions. In conclusion, the constructed dual-signal ratiometric ECL sensor reveals to perform enough anti-interference ability and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL response of the dual-signal ratiometric sensor to Co2+ ion concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
A dual-signal ratiometric ECL sensor for Co2+ ion was developed. As shown in Fig. 6 A, under the optimal conditions, the anodic ECL peak intensity (I a) around +1.2V increased with the increase of concentration of Co2+ ion while the cathodic ECL peak intensity(I c) around −0.75V descended. Taking the relative intensity change of I r (I r = I a/I c) as the function of the concentration of Co2+ ion, I r was consequently found to be linearly dependent on the square of Co2+ ion concentration in the range of 1.0μM to 70μM (Fig. 6B). The regression equation is I r =0.005C 2 +0.698 with a correlation coefficient of 0.987, where C is the value of Co2+ ion concentration. Meanwhile, the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.2μM (S/N
=
3). The results demonstrated that a ratiometric ECL strategy based on NGQDs dual-signal system was constructed with great potential for Co2+ ion detection. Comparison of the proposed method with other proposed methods [22,47–58] to determine Co2+ ions is shown in Table 1 . The detection limit is lower than that of most visual sensors and is comparable with the results obtained by potentiometry, CL, fluorescence, etc. Though some of other methods (such as ICP, ICP-OES and SERS) are more sensitive than the proposed method, they require tedious procedures for sample preparation and valuable equipment for operation. Hence, the proposed method holds the superiority prior for its convenient operation without needing time-consuming sample pretreatment and cheaper equipment. In conclusion, compared with the aforementioned detective methods for Co2+ ion, our method is economic, sensitive and simple.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the reliability of the proposed ratiometric ECL sensor in the practical samples, the content of Co2+ ion in different water samples was measured as examples, including spring water from Yuelu Mountain and river water from the Xiangjiang River in Changsha (China). All of the water samples were filtered through 0.22μm membranes and adjusted to 0.2M PBS solution (pH=8.0). No ECL intensity change occurs in both of the anodic and cathodic potential ECL in either spring water or river water sample. Hence, recovery measurements using spiked addition of different Co2+ ion were carried out and the results are listed in Table 2 . The recovery of the spiked samples obtained ranged from 95% to 103%, indicating that the present method was applicable for practical system detection.

In conclusion, this work has presented a novel single luminophor dual-potential ECL system for the fabrication of a convenient ratiometric sensor for Co2+ ion detection. During the cyclic potential scanning, the dissolved oxygen changes to O2
− and HO2 − which participate in the anodic and cathodic generating process of NGQDs. Co2+ ion is demonstrated to play the catalytic action of the intermediate steps of anodic ECL reactions to amplify the anodic intensity, and decrease the cathodic ECL intensity due to inhibition and quenching effects on the excited state of the luminophor. Hence, the target Co2+ ion is quantified by the ratio of ECL intensities at two excitation potentials. The constructed ratiaometric dual-potential ECL system shows excellent selectivity against other common metal ions. The proposed Co2+ ion modulated ECL strategy of NGQDs without extra oxidizing coreactant (such as H2O2, S2O8 2−) addition would pave a way for Co2+ relative sensor construction. As a single luminophor, NGQDs possessing multiple-ECL signals is straightforward without labeling, providing more reliable signals in sensing than traditional single ECL signal dependent strategies. Utilizing the dual-potential ECL sensor, the unavoidable uncertainty result relying on a single ECL signal previously could be largely avoided, making the sensing more credible in principle.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575038, 21235002, and 21305037), the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of NSFC (Grant 21521063), Young Top-notch Talent for Ten Thousand Talent Program, the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2015JJ1005), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.
